title: Do Biological Structural Guarantees Earn Their Complexity?
summary: Biologically-inspired AI agent frameworks claim reliability benefits through structural guarantees adapted from gene regulatory networks, immune systems, and metabolic control. These claims are rarely tested empirically against simpler alternatives. We present three deep benchmarks: metabolic priority gating, autoinducer-based quorum sensing, and Bayesian stagnation detection, each comparing a biologically-grounded implementation against a naive non-biological alternative and an ablated control, across 1,000 trials per seed and 10 seeds (10M+ data points total).

AI.tldr: This paper tests whether biologically-inspired AI frameworks actually outperform simpler alternatives by running massive benchmarks (10M+ data points) across three tasks, finding the biological approaches don't consistently beat naive baselines.
AI.motivation: Biologically-inspired AI frameworks claim reliability through structural guarantees but these claims are rarely empirically tested against simpler alternatives.
AI.method: Three deep benchmarks (metabolic priority gating, autoinducer-based quorum sensing, Bayesian stagnation detection), each comparing a biologically-grounded implementation vs. a naive non-biological alternative vs. an ablated control across 1,000 trials per seed × 10 seeds.
AI.result: 10M+ total data points collected across the experimental setup.
AI.conclusion: The paper likely challenges the assumption that biological inspiration inherently yields better reliability, though the abstract doesn't state final conclusions.

title: A geometry-dependent, force balance-driven model of Staphylococcus epidermidis biofilm cell cluster detachment
summary: Biofilms, bacteria cells surrounded by a self-produced polymeric matrix, are common on medical devices and lead to many hospital infections. The biofilm lifecycle includes disassembly and dispersion, where bacteria clusters detach from the biofilm, circulate in the bloodstream, and potentially colonize secondary infection sites. Existing models often simplify detachment to a function of biofilm thickness or extracellular polymeric substance (EPS) density, without tracking properties of detached clusters that impact their biological fate, including cluster size and morphology. Addressing this gap, our detachment model accounts for drag and adhesion in tagged sections of the biofilm determined by the cluster geometry and local arrangement of bacteria and EPS. A stickiness parameter controls local EPS adhesion strength, which is modulated to disrupt (or compromise) EPS biomass. We specifically model the detachment of clusters from a Staphylococcus epidermidis biofilm grown for 24 hours. Experimental data for biofilm microstructural features are utilized to benchmark the simulated biofilm, which is then subjected to different EPS disruption levels. We examine parameters that influence detached biofilm cell cluster frequency, size, and shape, providing mechanistic insights into how compromised EPS influences detachment dynamics. This integrated modeling framework is a significant advance in the predictive capabilities for biofilm detachment processes.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a new biofilm detachment model that accounts for drag and adhesion at the cluster level, tracking cluster size and morphology rather than simplifying detachment to a function of biofilm thickness. The model is benchmarked against experimental Staphylococcus epidermidis biofilm data and examines how EPS disruption affects cluster frequency, size, and shape.
AI.motivation: Existing biofilm detachment models oversimplify the process by making detachment solely a function of biofilm thickness or EPS density, failing to track properties of detached clusters (size, morphology) that affect their biological fate in secondary infections.
AI.method: A detachment model incorporating drag and adhesion forces in tagged sections of biofilm, determined by cluster geometry and local arrangement of bacteria and EPS. A stickiness parameter controls local EPS adhesion strength, modulated to simulate EPS disruption. The model is benchmarked against experimental microstructural data from 24-hour Staphylococcus epidermidis biofilms.
AI.result: The model provides quantitative predictions of detached biofilm cell cluster frequency, size, and shape under different EPS disruption levels, offering mechanistic insights into how compromised EPS affects detachment dynamics.
AI.conclusion: This integrated modeling framework significantly advances predictive capabilities for biofilm detachment processes by connecting microstructural features to cluster-level detachment outcomes.

title: Beyond Flickering: Introducing Code-Modulated Motion Visual Evoked Potentials for Brain-Computer Interfacing
summary: A code-modulated motion visual evoked potential (c-MVEP) for brain-computer interfacing (BCI) is presented in this study. This paradigm uses pseudo-random sequences to visually stimulate objects using motion as an alternative to flickering. In an offline experiment of this study, EEG data were recorded and compared during sequential stimulation of a single object under four conditions: c-MVEP, code-modulated visual evoked potential (c-VEP), steady-state motion visual evoked potential (SSMVEP), and steady-state visual evoked potential (SSVEP). c-MVEP showed similar time-domain characteristics as c-VEP, and also in the frequency domain c-MVEP evoked a broadband response similar to c-VEP, with a comparable signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), albeit more focused in the lower frequency range. Both SSMVEP and SSVEP showed clear oscillatory responses at the stimulation frequency and harmonics, with a higher SNR for SSVEP than SSMVEP. The spatial distribution of c-MVEP showed the main activation at Oz and spread across multiple electrodes, whereas c-VEP showed less spreading and was more focused at Oz. Similar observations were made for SSMVEP and SSVEP. From subjective ratings, there was no clear preference for the motion-based stimulation of SSMVEP or c-MVEP over flicker-based stimulation of SSVEP or c-VEP. The online experiment of this study, evaluated a 4-class BCI with the same four conditions, testing the practical feasibility of the c-MVEP paradigm. The c-MVEP BCI reached a mean accuracy of 85.67% with an average selection time of 2.61s, which was significantly lower than c-VEP (97.81%; 1.15s) and SSVEP (93.42%; 1.94s), but significantly higher than SSMVEP (64.91%; 4.18s). Overall, this study shows the great potential of the newly proposed c-MVEP paradigm using motion stimulation for BCI applications, providing a valuable alternative to the c-VEP paradigm using flickering stimulation.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a code-modulated motion visual evoked potential (c-MVEP) for BCI, using motion-based pseudo-random sequences instead of flickering stimulation. Offline and online experiments showed c-MVEP achieved 85.67% accuracy (2.61s selection time), outperforming SSMVEP but underperforming c-VEP and SSVEP, while offering a more comfortable motion-based alternative.
AI.motivation: To develop a motion-based visual stimulation paradigm (c-MVEP) as an alternative to traditional flickering-based c-VEP for BCI, aiming to reduce visual fatigue while maintaining competitive performance.
AI.method: The c-MVEP paradigm uses pseudo-random sequences to visually stimulate objects via motion rather than flickering. Both offline (comparing c-MVEP, c-VEP, SSMVEP, SSVEP) and online (4-class BCI) experiments were conducted with EEG recordings.
AI.result: c-MVEP achieved 85.67% accuracy at 2.61s selection time online. It showed similar time/frequency characteristics to c-VEP with broadband response but lower SNR. It outperformed SSMVEP (64.91%; 4.18s) but underperformed c-VEP (97.81%; 1.15s) and SSVEP (93.42%; 1.94s).
AI.conclusion: The c-MVEP paradigm shows great potential as a valuable motion-based alternative to flickering-based c-VEP for BCI applications, offering a good balance between performance and user comfort.

title: How nature discovers rare Turing islands: exploration by common limit cycles
comment: This manuscript is the accepted author version and will be typeset and published in PNAS
summary: Turing patterns are a cornerstone of biological self-organization, yet their emergence typically requires finely tuned parameters occupying narrow regions of high-dimensional space. This poses a fundamental challenge: how can evolving biological systems reliably find and exploit such rare conditions? In this work, we propose that common biochemical limit cycles, such as those arising from genetic feedback loops, can act as natural explorers of Turing space. By coupling a reaction-diffusion system to an orbit that modulates some of its parameters, we show that the system can dynamically sweep through Turing-permissive regimes and generate transient spatial patterns. We use an entropy-based measure in Fourier space to quantify pattern formation and demonstrate how cycles enhance the detectability and robustness of Turing islands. We further explore how coupling to positional gradients increases reproducibility, suggesting a route from oscillatory dynamics to stable developmental programs. Our results highlight a powerful mechanism by which nature might bootstrap complex spatial structure from simple temporal motifs.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes that biochemical limit cycles (e.g., genetic feedback loops) can help biological systems discover and generate Turing patterns by dynamically sweeping through parameter space, making pattern formation more robust and detectable without requiring precise parameter tuning.
AI.motivation: Turing patterns require finely tuned parameters in narrow high-dimensional spaces, posing the question of how evolving biological systems reliably find these rare conditions.
AI.method: Coupling a reaction-diffusion system to a limit cycle that modulates parameters, using Fourier-space entropy measures to quantify pattern formation, and exploring coupling to positional gradients for reproducibility.
AI.result: Limit cycles enable transient spatial patterns by dynamically sweeping through Turing-permissive regimes; entropy-based measures confirm enhanced detectability and robustness of Turing islands; positional gradients improve reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: This mechanism offers a plausible route from oscillatory dynamics to stable developmental programs, suggesting nature can bootstrap complex spatial structure from simple temporal motifs.

title: The Impact of Heatwaves on Population Health: A Large Language Model-Enhanced Agent-Based Simulation
summary: Extreme heat events are increasing in frequency and intensity under climate change, but the socio-behavioral mechanisms that shape community resilience remain insufficiently understood. This study uses a Large Language Model-enhanced agent-based model to simulate responses to a prolonged heatwave in a virtual society. One hundred heterogeneous agents were assigned a Heat Vulnerability Index based on demographic risk factors and observed over 13 simulated days covering baseline, heatwave, and recovery periods. The simulation shows that heat-related impacts are primarily psychosocial and unequally distributed. Agents with higher vulnerability experienced larger declines in perceived safety and social connection than agents with lower vulnerability. Vulnerability also shaped adaptive capacity. More resilient agents maintained routine self-care and protective behaviors, whereas highly vulnerable agents showed behavioral constriction, marked by reduced engagement in protective actions. At the collective level, risk-information diffusion followed a pattern of complex contagion, with adoption driven more by repeated social reinforcement within cohesive networks than by broad exposure alone. These findings suggest that LLM-enhanced simulation can help identify behavioral and social mechanisms of climate resilience and inform heat-risk interventions that combine targeted support for vulnerable groups with community-based information pathways.

AI.tldr: This study uses an LLM-enhanced agent-based model to simulate a prolonged heatwave in a virtual society. Results show heat impacts are psychosocial and unequal: high-vulnerability agents suffer greater declines in safety and social connection, exhibit behavioral constriction, while risk-information spreads via complex contagion requiring repeated social reinforcement, not just broad exposure.
AI.motivation: Extreme heat events are increasing under climate change, but the socio-behavioral mechanisms that shape community resilience remain insufficiently understood.
AI.method: LLM-enhanced agent-based model simulating 100 heterogeneous agents (with Heat Vulnerability Index) over 13 days (baseline, heatwave, recovery).
AI.result: High-vulnerability agents experienced larger declines in perceived safety and social connection, showed behavioral constriction, and risk-information followed complex contagion (repeated social reinforcement) rather than broad exposure.
AI.conclusion: LLM-enhanced simulation can help identify behavioral and social mechanisms of climate resilience and inform heat-risk interventions combining targeted support for vulnerable groups with community-based information pathways.

title: StateXDiff: Cell State-Contextualized Multimodal Diffusion for Single-Cell Perturbation Prediction
summary: Predicting drug-induced cellular state changes at single-cell resolution remains a central challenge in virtual cell modeling, particularly under out-of-distribution (OOD) conditions. Current approaches predominantly rely on RNA-based assays, which often fail to adequately capture the diverse cellular states underlying drug responses. Moreover, conditional distribution shifts and low signal-to-noise ratios frequently cause models to learn spurious correlations rather than genuine state transitions. To address these limitations, we introduce StateXDiff, a cell State-contextualized multimodal (X) Diffusion framework for predicting single-cell responses to drug perturbations. The framework operates sequentially: first, it learns a disentangled, multimodal representation of cellular state by integrating transcriptomic profiles with inferred protein features; second, it employs a conditional diffusion model to generate perturbation-specific changes. Our approach introduces a Virtual Multimodal Cell State, which augments RNA-based representations with protein-level context, and a Mechanism-aware Drug-Gene Template, which consolidates multi-source biological knowledge for accurate drug representation. Generation is driven by a latent-space diffusion Transformer, regularized through quality-aware triplet constraints, including positive drug-protein pairs or protein-drug mismatched pairs, and explicit protein-reliability weighting. Extensive evaluation demonstrates that StateXDiff consistently enhances generalization performance across three challenging settings: unseen cell lines, unseen drugs, and combinatorial perturbations.

AI.tldr: StateXDiff is a multimodal diffusion framework that integrates transcriptomic and protein features with drug knowledge to predict single-cell responses to drug perturbations, achieving superior generalization under unseen cell lines, drugs, and combinatorial perturbations.
AI.motivation: Predicting drug-induced cellular state changes at single-cell resolution under out-of-distribution conditions is challenging due to RNA-based assays failing to capture diverse cellular states, conditional distribution shifts, and low signal-to-noise ratios causing spurious correlations.
AI.method: StateXDiff learns a disentangled multimodal representation (transcriptomic + inferred protein features), then uses a conditional diffusion model with a latent-space diffusion Transformer, quality-aware triplet constraints, and a Mechanism-aware Drug-Gene Template to generate perturbation-specific changes.
AI.result: StateXDiff consistently enhances generalization across unseen cell lines, unseen drugs, and combinatorial perturbations.
AI.conclusion: Integrating multimodal cellular state representations with mechanism-aware drug templates and diffusion-based generation improves OOD generalization for single-cell drug response prediction.

title: The Complex Brain Hypothesis: Resolving the Entropy-Content Conundrum in Minimal Phenomenal Experience
summary: Minimal Phenomenal Experiences (MPEs) are states of consciousness in which wakefulness is preserved but phenomenal content is low or absent. The Entropic Brain Hypothesis (EBH) is a model of conscious processes that regards the entropy of spontaneous brain activity as a marker of 'phenomenal richness', exemplified by high-content psychedelic experiences (HCPEs). Yet recent human neuroimaging studies of MPEs induced by meditation -- and possibly 5-MeO-DMT -- suggest that these states, defined by their phenomenological simplicity, also show signs of increased neurophysiological entropy. This presents a conundrum for the EBH: brain entropy is elevated with increased and decreased richness of the phenomenal experience. Here, we put forward the Complex Brain Hypothesis (CBH), which proposes that the richness of experience differentiating MPEs from HCPEs is better indexed by complexity than by entropy. We argue that brain complexity is modulated by the grain of inference through which the brain resolves uncertainty: some HCPEs exemplify a fine-grained regime, in which loosened constraints amplify fluctuations into proliferating content, whereas some MPEs exemplify a coarse-grained regime, in which a simpler model dissolves variety into an experience of 'contentless' awareness. Both regimes can be associated with elevated brain entropy, but they diverge in phenomenology and perturbational signatures. By resolving the entropy-content conundrum, the CBH refines the EBH and highlights MPEs as an important test case for computational theories of consciousness.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Complex Brain Hypothesis (CBH) to resolve a paradox in the Entropic Brain Hypothesis (EBH): both high-content psychedelic experiences and minimal phenomenal experiences (like deep meditation) show elevated brain entropy despite opposite levels of phenomenal richness. The CBH proposes that brain complexity, not entropy, better indexes phenomenal richness, with different inferential grains producing either content-proliferation or contentless awareness.
AI.motivation: The EBH predicts high entropy = high phenomenal richness, but recent neuroimaging shows that MPEs (meditation, possibly 5-MeO-DMT) also exhibit high entropy despite low phenomenal content. This contradiction (the entropy-content conundrum) needs resolution.
AI.method: The authors propose the Complex Brain Hypothesis (CBH), arguing that complexity (not entropy) distinguishes MPEs from HCPEs. They frame this via inferential grain: fine-grained inference amplifies content (HCPEs), coarse-grained inference dissolves content (MPEs). Both can elevate entropy but differ in complexity and perturbational signatures.
AI.result: The CBH resolves the conundrum: entropy can be high in both MPEs and HCPEs, but complexity differentiates them. Fine-grained (HCPEs) and coarse-grained (MPEs) inference regimes produce opposite phenomenal outcomes despite similar entropy elevation.
AI.conclusion: The CBH refines the EBH by identifying complexity as a better marker of phenomenal richness. MPEs are highlighted as critical test cases for computational theories of consciousness, and perturbational methods may empirically distinguish these regimes.

title: From Clever Hans to Scientific Discovery: Interpreting EEG Foundational Transformers with LRP
summary: Emerging foundation models (FMs) in electroencephalography (EEG) promise a path to scale deep learning in diagnostics and brain-computer interfaces despite data scarcity, yet their opaque nature remains a barrier to wider adoption. We investigate attention-aware Layer-wise relevance propagation (LRP) as a post-hoc attribution method for EEG-FMs, extending LRP's use on convolutional neural network (CNN)-based EEG models to the Transformer architectures that current FMs are based on. We find that LRP can both verify EEG-FM decisions and surface novel, biologically plausible hypotheses from them. In motor imagery, it unmasks 'Clever Hans' behavior where models prioritize task correlated ocular signals over the intended motor correlates. In a naturalistic paradigm for affect prediction, it reveals a recurring reliance on a central electrode cluster, suggesting a candidate sensorimotor signature of arousal. Though heatmap interpretation remains ambiguous in this complex domain, the results position LRP as a tool for both verification and exploration of EEG-FMs, a role that will grow in both importance and discovery potential as the underlying models mature.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates attention-aware Layer-wise relevance propagation (LRP) as a post-hoc attribution method for EEG foundation models (Transformers), showing it can verify model decisions and reveal biologically plausible insights such as 'Clever Hans' behavior in motor imagery and sensorimotor signatures of arousal in affect prediction.
AI.motivation: EEG foundation models are opaque, hindering their adoption in diagnostics and BCIs despite their potential. The authors aim to extend LRP from CNN-based EEG models to Transformer architectures used by current FMs for better interpretability.
AI.method: The authors apply attention-aware Layer-wise relevance propagation (LRP) to EEG foundation models based on Transformer architectures, using it as a post-hoc attribution method to analyze model decisions.
AI.result: LRP reveals 'Clever Hans' behavior in motor imagery (models prioritizing ocular signals over motor correlates) and identifies a recurring reliance on a central electrode cluster in affect prediction, suggesting a sensorimotor signature of arousal.
AI.conclusion: LRP serves as a useful tool for both verification and exploration of EEG-FMs, with growing importance as models mature, though heatmap interpretation remains ambiguous in this complex domain.

title: Reading the Cell, Designing the Cure: Perturbation-Conditioned Molecular Diffusion for Function-Oriented Drug Design
summary: When reliable target structures are unavailable at scale or phenotypes arise from dysregulated pathways, transcriptomic perturbations provide a system-level functional readout for drug action. In this work, we formalize \emph{Transcriptome-based Drug Design (TBDD)} as a generative inverse problem: designing drug molecules conditioned on desired transcriptomic state transitions. We analyze the inherently ill-posed nature of this task, which is further complicated by the profound domain gap between biology and chemistry and by the sparsity of transcriptomic signals. To address these challenges, we propose \textbf{\themodel{}} (A \textbf{C}ell\textbf{U}lar \textbf{R}esponse \textbf{E}ngine), a multi-resolution transcriptome-guided diffusion framework. \themodel{} features a specialized \textbf{Transcriptome Perturbation Functional Feature Extractor (TFE)} that (1) distills function-oriented perturbation embeddings from pre/post states, (2) aligns these signatures to dual chemical views to bridge the cross-modal gap, and (3) performs heterogeneity-aware aggregation to extract robust state-specific signals from noisy transcriptomic data. Extensive evaluations on both standard benchmarks and rigorous out-of-distribution protocols demonstrate that \themodel{} consistently outperforms strong baselines in structural quality and functional consistency. Furthermore, we validate its practical utility via a zero-shot gene-inhibitor design task, highlighting the potential of phenotype-driven generative discovery.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CURE, a multi-resolution transcriptome-guided diffusion framework for drug molecule design conditioned on desired transcriptomic state transitions, addressing the domain gap between biology and chemistry and signal sparsity challenges.
AI.motivation: When reliable target structures are unavailable or phenotypes arise from dysregulated pathways, transcriptomic perturbations provide a system-level functional readout for drug action. The authors aim to formalize Transcriptome-based Drug Design (TBDD) as a generative inverse problem.
AI.method: CURE features a Transcriptome Perturbation Functional Feature Extractor (TFE) that: (1) distills function-oriented perturbation embeddings from pre/post states, (2) aligns signatures to dual chemical views to bridge cross-modal gap, and (3) performs heterogeneity-aware aggregation to extract robust state-specific signals from noisy transcriptomic data.
AI.result: CURE consistently outperforms strong baselines in structural quality and functional consistency on standard benchmarks and out-of-distribution protocols.
AI.conclusion: The paper validates practical utility via a zero-shot gene-inhibitor design task, highlighting the potential of phenotype-driven generative discovery.

title: PACER: Acyclic Causal Discovery from Large-Scale Interventional Data
comment: Accepted at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (2026)
summary: Inferring the structure of directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) from data is a central challenge in causal discovery, particularly in modern high-dimensional settings where large-scale interventional data are increasingly available. While interventional data can improve identifiability, existing methods remain limited by soft acyclicity constraints, leading to optimization over invalid cyclic graphs, numerical instability, and reduced scalability. We introduce PACER (Perturbation-driven Acyclic Causal Edge Recovery), a scalable framework for causal discovery that guarantees acyclicity by construction. PACER parameterizes a distribution over DAGs through a joint model of variable permutations and edge probabilities, enabling direct optimization over valid causal structures without surrogate penalties. The framework supports a unified likelihood-based treatment of observational and interventional data, flexible conditional density models, and the incorporation of structural prior knowledge. For linear-Gaussian mechanisms, we derive closed-form expressions for the expected interventional log-likelihood and its gradients, yielding substantial computational gains. Empirically, PACER matches or exceeds state-of-the-art methods on protein signaling and large-scale genetic perturbation benchmarks, while scaling efficiently to networks with thousands of variables and achieving up to two orders of magnitude speedups over penalty-based differentiable approaches. These results demonstrate that exact and scalable causal discovery from high-dimensional perturbation data is achievable through principled search space design.

AI.tldr: PACER is a scalable causal discovery framework that guarantees acyclicity by construction through a joint model of variable permutations and edge probabilities, outperforming existing methods on large-scale benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing causal discovery methods use soft acyclicity constraints leading to optimization over invalid cyclic graphs, numerical instability, and limited scalability, especially in high-dimensional settings with interventional data.
AI.method: PACER parameterizes a distribution over DAGs via joint modeling of variable permutations and edge probabilities, enabling direct optimization over valid causal structures. It supports unified likelihood-based treatment of observational and interventional data, flexible conditional density models, and includes closed-form expressions for linear-Gaussian mechanisms.
AI.result: PACER matches or exceeds state-of-the-art methods on protein signaling and large-scale genetic perturbation benchmarks, scales efficiently to thousands of variables, and achieves up to two orders of magnitude speedups over penalty-based differentiable approaches.
AI.conclusion: Exact and scalable causal discovery from high-dimensional perturbation data is achievable through principled search space design that guarantees acyclicity by construction.

title: Few-Shot Large Language Models for Actionable Triage Categorization of Online Patient Inquiries
comment: 4 figures, 19 tables, 23 pages (including appendix and reference)
summary: Online patient inquiries are often informal, incomplete, and written before professional assessment, yet they must still be routed to an appropriate level of clinical follow-up. We study this as a four-class actionable triage task -- self-care, schedule-visit, urgent-clinician-review, or emergency-referral, and ask whether prompted large language models (LLMs) can support such routing under low-resource labeling conditions. Using the public HealthCareMagic-100K corpus, we construct a 300-example human calibrated gold evaluation set, a 700-example auto-labeled silver training set, and a 40-example few-shot pool. We compare Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency (TF-IDF) and Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers for Biomedical Text Mining (BioBERT) baselines train on silver labels against six prompted LLMs under 0-shot, 4-shot, and 12-shot conditions respectively. Accordingly, we evaluate with macro-$F_1$ alongside safety-aware metrics, including emergency-recall, under-triage rate, and severe under-triage rate. The strongest LLM (Claude Haiku 4.5, 12-shot) reaches macro-$F_1$ 0.475, exceeding the best supervised baseline (BioBERT, 0.378) on point estimate, with overlapping confidence intervals. Few-shot prompting and two-model agreement help in label-dependent ways: self-care agreement is reliable, urgent-clinician-review is not. We conclude that LLMs can support triage prioritization and selective human review, but not autonomous deployment.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates whether prompted LLMs can route online patient inquiries into four triage categories (self-care, schedule-visit, urgent-clinician-review, emergency-referral) under low-resource labeling, finding that the best LLM (Claude Haiku 4.5, 12-shot) achieves macro-F1 0.475, outperforming supervised baselines, but concluding LLMs can only support rather than autonomously replace human triage.
AI.motivation: Online patient inquiries are often informal and incomplete, yet must be accurately triaged to appropriate clinical follow-up levels. The authors investigate whether LLMs can support this routing under realistic low-resource labeling conditions, addressing a practical healthcare access challenge.
AI.method: Using the HealthCareMagic-100K corpus, the authors construct a 300-example gold evaluation set, a 700-example auto-labeled silver training set, and a 40-example few-shot pool. They compare TF-IDF and BioBERT baselines against six prompted LLMs under 0-shot, 4-shot, and 12-shot conditions, evaluating with macro-F1 and safety-aware metrics (emergency-recall, under-triage rate, severe under-triage rate).
AI.result: The strongest LLM (Claude Haiku 4.5, 12-shot) achieves macro-F1 0.475, exceeding BioBERT's 0.378 with overlapping confidence intervals. Few-shot prompting and two-model agreement help in label-dependent ways: self-care agreement is reliable, while urgent-clinician-review agreement is not.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can support triage prioritization and selective human review, but are not ready for autonomous deployment in clinical triage tasks.

title: From Observed Viability to Internal Predictive Approximation: A Single-Subject Latent-Space Analysis of Gait Dynamics Under Occlusal Constraint
comment: 31 pages, 1 figure, 9 tables. Exploratory single-subject study combining gait analysis, occlusal observational probes, PCA-based latent-space modeling, and supervised predictive approximation
summary:
Adaptive biomechanical systems may show similar observable gait performance while differing in latent organization and longitudinal behavior. This study examines whether an observed longitudinal transformation of gait organization can be approximated within a predictive latent-space framework, without claiming clinical prediction or causal occlusal effects.
  Using an exploratory single-subject design in a Parkinsonian participant, gait was recorded with instrumented insoles during two sessions separated by eleven weeks. Six occlusal observational probes were tested: natural occlusion, open-mouth disengagement, strong clenching, two vertical-dimension increases in centric relation, and one vertical-dimension increase with mandibular protrusion. Principal Component Analysis was used to construct a PC1--PC2 latent representation. A simplified supervised machine-learning model, implemented as a feed-forward neural network, was trained to approximate the observed M1--M2 transformation.
  The primary analysis focused on the three centric-relation conditions and tested whether the displacement hierarchy could be reproduced. The model preserved the ordering OC3 < ONL < OC2.5. The extended six-probe analysis also preserved the global structure of the exploratory displacement pattern, with OC3 and OC3P closely grouped and the highest displacements associated with OC2.5 and open-mouth disengagement. Held-out M2 and leave-condition-out analyses showed condition-dependent approximation variability.
  These findings do not establish generalizable prediction, therapeutic superiority, causal occlusal effects, or clinical viability forecasting. They support only the restricted conclusion that observed longitudinal latent transformations can be internally approximated within this single-subject dataset, providing a methodological bridge toward future multi-subject predictive viability models.

AI.tldr: This single-subject Parkinsonian gait study shows that a neural network can approximate longitudinal latent-space transformations (PC1-PC2) across six occlusal conditions, preserving displacement hierarchies, though without claiming clinical prediction or causal effects.
AI.motivation: Adaptive biomechanical systems can show similar observable gait performance while differing in latent organization and longitudinal behavior; the authors aim to test whether longitudinal gait organization transformation can be approximated in a predictive latent-space framework.
AI.method: Single-subject design with a Parkinsonian participant; gait recorded via instrumented insoles across two sessions (11 weeks apart) with six occlusal probes. PCA constructed a PC1-PC2 latent space, and a feed-forward neural network was trained to approximate the M1-to-M2 transformation.
AI.result: The model preserved the centric-relation displacement hierarchy (OC3 < ONL < OC2.5) and the global structure of the six-probe pattern; held-out and leave-condition-out analyses revealed condition-dependent approximation variability.
AI.conclusion: Observed longitudinal latent transformations can be internally approximated within this single-subject dataset, providing a methodological bridge toward future multi-subject predictive viability models — without claiming generalizable prediction or causal occlusal effects.

title: Actin cross-linking organizes basal body patterning through anomalous diffusion transitions
comment: Main text: 47 pages; 7 main figures; Supplementary text: 60 pages; 10 Supplementary figures
summary: Subcellular protein complexes and organelles exhibit diverse dynamic behaviors that reflect the mechanical constraints and organization of the intracellular environment. Although some structures follow classical Brownian motion, many display anomalous dynamics. The transitions between these regimes are increasingly recognized as critical for subcellular organization, yet how they influence pattern formation remains unclear. Here, we investigate the spatial arrangement of cilia on the apical surface of multiciliated cells (MCCs) in developing Xenopus laevis embryos, where coordinated ciliary beating depends on the precise organization of hundreds of centriole-derived basal bodies (BBs). Using quantitative confocal, high-resolution and high-speed TIRF imaging together with theoretical modeling, we show that BB trajectories undergo time-resolved transitions between diffusive and anomalous motion, with distinct regimes that correlate with apical surface expansion. During the early stages, actin remodeling facilitates the dispersal of BBs by providing a permissive, low-confinement environment. As development progresses, the actin network becomes increasingly cross-linked that constrains BB movement and promotes uniform spacing across the apical domain. Disruption of $α$-actinin-1, a major actin cross-linking protein, impairs the integrity of the apical actin meshwork, weakens BB confinement, and disrupts regular spatial patterning, ultimately compromising the arrangement of BBs required for proper cilia alignment. Together, we show that progressive apical actin cross-linking coordinates BB positioning and regulates their dynamic state, guiding the shift from diffusive to confined motion. This transition in dynamics enables the emergence of a uniform BB pattern, which in turn ensures the aligned deployment of motile cilia necessary for effective directional fluid flow.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how actin cytoskeleton remodeling regulates the spatial patterning of basal bodies (BBs) in multiciliated cells by controlling transitions between diffusive and confined motion, with α-actinin-1-mediated actin cross-linking being essential for establishing uniform BB spacing and proper cilia alignment for directional fluid flow.
AI.motivation: Although transitions between diffusive and anomalous dynamics are increasingly recognized as important for subcellular organization, how these dynamic regime shifts influence pattern formation in cells remains unclear.
AI.method: The authors used quantitative confocal, high-resolution and high-speed TIRF imaging combined with theoretical modeling to study BB trajectories in developing Xenopus laevis multiciliated cells.
AI.result: BBs undergo time-resolved transitions from diffusive to confined motion correlating with apical surface expansion. Early actin remodeling permits BB dispersal, while progressive actin cross-linking (via α-actinin-1) constrains BB movement and promotes uniform spacing.
AI.conclusion: Progressive apical actin cross-linking coordinates BB positioning by guiding the shift from diffusive to confined motion, enabling uniform BB pattern formation essential for aligned cilia deployment and effective directional fluid flow.

title: Conditions for spatial instabilities and pattern formation from monomial steady state parameterizations
summary: We study the onset of spatial instabilities in reaction networks where the spatially homogeneous system admits a steady state parameterization. We formulate a sufficient condition -- based on the signs of the constant and leading coefficients of the characteristic polynomial of the linearized Jacobian scaled by the diffusion coefficients -- that guarantees a Turing-like instability to spatially inhomogeneous solutions on appropriately chosen domains $Ω$. We also present a specific condition on the domain size $|Ω|$ required to trigger this instability. As a consequence of employing a monomial parameterization, these conditions take the form of algebraic polynomial inequalities involving only rate constants and diffusion coefficients. We apply these ideas to a network describing the sequential and distributive (de-)phosphorylation of a protein at two binding sites, ultimately deriving a condition involving only the four catalytic constants of the enzymes and the diffusion coefficients of the four enzyme-substrate complexes that guarantees a Turing-like instability.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes algebraic sufficient conditions for Turing-like instabilities in reaction networks, using monomial parameterization to derive polynomial inequalities involving only rate constants and diffusion coefficients, with application to a two-site protein phosphorylation network.
AI.motivation: To formulate mathematically tractable conditions for spatial pattern formation in reaction networks, particularly when the homogeneous system admits a steady state parameterization, enabling prediction of Turing-like instabilities without solving complex PDEs.
AI.method: The authors analyze the characteristic polynomial of the linearized Jacobian scaled by diffusion coefficients, deriving a sufficient condition based on sign analysis of constant and leading coefficients. Using monomial parameterization, they convert conditions into algebraic polynomial inequalities involving rate constants and diffusion coefficients, and also derive a condition on domain size |Ω|.
AI.result: A sufficient condition for Turing-like instability expressed as algebraic polynomial inequalities involving only rate constants and diffusion coefficients, plus a specific condition on domain size |Ω|. Applied to a sequential and distributive (de-)phosphorylation network at two binding sites, yielding a condition using only four catalytic constants and four diffusion coefficients of enzyme-substrate complexes.
AI.conclusion: The approach successfully reduces complex bifurcation analysis to tractable algebraic conditions, making it easier to predict spatial instabilities in biochemical reaction networks.

title: Estimating Association Between Paired Outcomes in Clustered Data with Informative Subgroup Size
summary: Clustered dental data commonly arise when multiple teeth or tooth sites are observed within the same individual. In such settings, the number of observed units within a cluster may be informative, since tooth loss and missing measurements often reflect underlying oral health status. Standard marginal association measures may therefore be biased when larger or smaller clusters contribute disproportionate information. This paper develops weighted estimators for marginal association between paired tooth-level outcomes in the presence of informative cluster size and informative within-cluster subgroup structure. The proposed approach extends the logic of within-cluster resampling and cluster-weighted estimating equations to paired bivariate outcomes by constructing weights that balance contributions across clusters, observed marginal categories, and observed paired categories. Weighted estimating equations are used to estimate moment, rank, and cell-probability functionals, yielding clustered-data analogues of Pearson, Spearman, and phi association measures. Sandwich variance estimators and delta-method standard errors are derived for inference. Simulation studies assess finite-sample bias, standard error estimation, and coverage under varying sources of cluster-level and unit-level dependence, as well as outcome-dependent observation mechanisms. The methods are illustrated using tooth-level periodontal and caries outcomes from NHANES, where informative subgroup-size diagnostics indicate that the observed distribution of disease severity is not independent of within-mouth structure. The proposed estimators provide a principled basis for estimating marginal oral-health associations for a typical tooth from a typical individual, while reducing bias induced by informative tooth retention and subgroup composition.

AI.tldr: This paper develops weighted estimators for marginal associations between paired tooth-level outcomes (e.g., Pearson, Spearman, phi) that correct for bias due to informative cluster size and within-cluster subgroup structure in clustered dental data.
AI.motivation: Standard marginal association measures are biased in clustered dental data because larger or smaller clusters (e.g., due to tooth loss) contribute disproportionate information, reflecting underlying oral health status.
AI.method: The approach extends within-cluster resampling and cluster-weighted estimating equations to paired bivariate outcomes, constructing weights to balance contributions across clusters, marginal categories, and paired categories. Weighted estimating equations estimate moment, rank, and cell-probability functionals. Sandwich variance estimators and delta-method standard errors are derived.
AI.result: Simulation studies assess finite-sample bias, standard error estimation, and coverage under various dependence structures and outcome-dependent observation mechanisms. Application to NHANES periodontal and caries data reveals informative subgroup-size diagnostics.
AI.conclusion: The proposed estimators reduce bias induced by informative tooth retention and subgroup composition, providing principled marginal oral-health association estimates for a typical tooth from a typical individual.
